title: Angles and triangles
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 9 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 7 Geometry, Section 7A
keyIdeas: Acute: 0°-90°. Right: 90°. Obtuse: 90°-180°. Straight: 180°. Reflex: 180°-360°. Revolution: 360°.
Complementary angles sum to 90°. Supplementary angles sum to 180°. Angles at a point (revolution) sum to 360°. Vertically opposite angles are equal.
The angle sum of a triangle is 180°.
Exterior angle theorem: the exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two opposite (remote) interior angles.

Triangle types by side length: scalene (no equal sides), isosceles (two equal sides/angles), equilateral (all sides/angles equal, all 60°). By angle: acute (all angles < 90°), right-angled (one 90° angle), obtuse (one angle > 90°).

workedExamples.title: Example 1 — Finding supplementary and complementary angles
workedExamples.question: For an angle of size 47°, determine the size of its (a) supplementary angle and (b) complementary angle.
workedExamples.solution:
(a) 180° − 47° = 133° — supplementary angles sum to 180°.
(b) 90° − 47° = 43° — complementary angles sum to 90°.

workedExamples.title: Example 2 — Finding unknown angles in triangles
workedExamples.question: Name the type of triangle and find the pronumeral: (a) angles 60°, 60°, x°  (b) an isosceles triangle with apex 120° and two equal base angles r°  (c) angles s°, 50°, 60°.
workedExamples.solution:
(a) Equilateral triangle. x = 60 — all sides equal, so all angles equal.
(b) Obtuse isosceles triangle. 2r + 120 = 180 → 2r = 60 → r = 30. Angles in a triangle sum to 180°; since it's isosceles both base angles are r°.
(c) Acute scalene triangle. s + 50 + 60 = 180 → s + 110 = 180 → s = 70.

workedExamples.title: Example 3 — Finding exterior and other angles
workedExamples.question: Find the pronumeral: (a) an exterior angle y° next to a triangle with interior angles 90° and 55°  (b) two triangles sharing a vertex where a 47° base angle appears twice via an isosceles triangle and vertically opposite angles, find x°.
workedExamples.solution:
(a) y = 90 + 55 = 145 — exterior angle theorem: the exterior angle equals the sum of the two opposite (remote) interior angles. (Alternatively: the third interior angle is 180−90−55=35°, and y is supplementary to it: y=180−35=145.)
(b) x + 47 + 47 = 180 → x + 94 = 180 → x = 86 — uses the isosceles triangle's equal base angles and that vertically opposite angles are equal.

questions.prompt: A 90° angle is called a _____ angle.

misconceptions.reason: confuses right (90°) with straight (180°)

questions.prompt: A _____ angle is called a straight angle.

misconceptions.reason: confuses straight (180°) with right (90°)

questions.prompt: A 360° angle is called a _____.

misconceptions.reason: confuses revolution (exactly 360°) with reflex (between 180° and 360°)

questions.prompt: _____ angles are between 90° and 180°.

misconceptions.reason: confuses obtuse (90°-180°) with reflex (180°-360°)

questions.prompt: _____ angles are between 0° and 90°.
Reflex angles are between _____ and 360°.
Complementary angles sum to _____.

misconceptions.reason: swaps complementary (90°) with supplementary (180°)
swaps supplementary (180°) with complementary (90°)

questions.prompt: The three angles in a triangle sum to _____.

misconceptions.reason: confuses triangle angle sum (180°) with a revolution or quadrilateral sum (360°)

questions.prompt: Vertically opposite angles are _____.

misconceptions.reason: confuses vertically opposite angles (equal) with the adjacent angles on the same line (which are supplementary)

questions.prompt: Is the pair of angles 30° and 60° supplementary (S), complementary (C) or neither (N)?

misconceptions.reason: didn't add the pair first (30+60=90, not 180) before classifying

questions.prompt: Is the pair of angles 45° and 135° supplementary (S), complementary (C) or neither (N)?

misconceptions.reason: didn't check that 45+135 = 180

questions.prompt: Is the pair of angles 100° and 90° supplementary (S), complementary (C) or neither (N)?

misconceptions.reason: assumed any two 'large-looking' angles must be supplementary without adding them (100+90=190, not 180)

questions.prompt: Is the pair of angles 50° and 40° supplementary (S), complementary (C) or neither (N)?
For an angle of size 72°, find (a) its supplementary angle and (b) its complementary angle.

misconceptions.reason: swapped supplementary and complementary

questions.prompt: For an angle of 55°, find its supplementary angle and its complementary angle.

misconceptions.reason: swapped the two definitions

questions.prompt: For an angle of 74°, find its supplementary angle and its complementary angle.
For an angle of 89°, find its supplementary angle and its complementary angle.

misconceptions.reason: arithmetic slip subtracting close to 90, or forgot to subtract at all

questions.prompt: For an angle of 22°, find its supplementary angle and its complementary angle.
For an angle of 38°, find its supplementary angle and its complementary angle.
For an angle of 47°, find its supplementary angle and its complementary angle.
A triangle has angles 100°, 40° and a°. Find a and name the type of triangle.
questions.answer: a = 40 (180-100-40). Isosceles (two 40° angles, so two equal sides).

misconceptions.reason: only subtracted one of the two given angles from 180, or subtracted from 360 instead of 180
named the triangle type without noticing the two equal angles (40° and 40°) implied by their own answer

questions.prompt: A triangle has angles 80°, 70° and b°. Find b and name the type of triangle.
questions.answer: b = 30 (180-80-70). Acute scalene (all three angles different and all less than 90°).

misconceptions.reason: subtracted only one given angle from 180 instead of both

questions.prompt: A triangle has angles 40°, c° and 50°. Find c and name the type of triangle.

misconceptions.reason: computed c=90 correctly but didn't recognise that a 90° angle makes it right-angled

questions.prompt: A triangle has angles 30°, g° and 50°. Find g and name the type of triangle.
questions.answer: g = 100 (180-30-50). Obtuse scalene triangle (one angle over 90°, all three different).

misconceptions.reason: found g=100 correctly but forgot that an angle over 90° makes the triangle obtuse, not acute

questions.prompt: A triangle has angles 60°, h° and 60°. Find h and name the type of triangle.

misconceptions.reason: correctly found h=60 but didn't notice all three angles are equal, which makes it equilateral, not just isosceles

questions.prompt: A triangle has angles 35°, 75° and i°. Find i and name the type of triangle.

misconceptions.reason: subtracted only one of the two given angles from 180

questions.prompt: How many degrees does the minute hand of a clock rotate in 1 hour?
How many degrees does the minute hand of a clock rotate in 1/4 of an hour?
How many degrees does the minute hand of a clock rotate in 10 minutes?

misconceptions.reason: used 1°/minute instead of the correct rate of 6°/minute (360°/60min)

questions.prompt: How many degrees does the minute hand of a clock rotate in 15 minutes?
How many degrees does the minute hand of a clock rotate in 72 minutes?

misconceptions.reason: didn't realise 72 minutes is more than one revolution, so the answer can exceed 360°

questions.prompt: How many degrees does the minute hand of a clock rotate in 1 minute?
How many degrees does the minute hand of a clock rotate in 2 hours?
How many degrees does the minute hand of a clock rotate in 1 day?
Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 3:00 p.m.
Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 5:00 a.m.
Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 6:30 p.m.
questions.answer: 15° (the hour hand has moved halfway from 6 to 7 by 6:30)

misconceptions.reason: assumed the hour hand sits exactly on the 6 at 6:30, ignoring that it moves 0.5°/minute between numbers

questions.prompt: Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 11:30 p.m.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: treated the hour hand as fixed on 11
misconceptions.reason: forgot the hour hand moves between numbers, so at 11:30 it is halfway between 11 and 12

questions.prompt: Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 3:45 a.m.
Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 1:20 a.m.
Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 4:55 a.m.

misconceptions.reason: gave the reflex angle (the long way around) instead of the smaller angle between the hands (should not exceed 180°)

questions.prompt: Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 2:42 a.m.
Find the angle between the hour and minute hands at 9:27 a.m.

Prove that the three exterior angles of a triangle sum to 360°. (Use the fact that the three interior angles sum to 180°, and that each interior angle and its exterior angle are supplementary/sum to 180° since they sit on a straight line.)
questions.answer: Let the interior angles be a, b, c with a+b+c=180. Each exterior angle is 180° minus its adjacent interior angle, so the three exterior angles are (180-a), (180-b) and (180-c). Their sum is 540-(a+b+c) = 540-180 = 360°.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: just states '360° because it's a revolution' with no derivation
misconceptions.reason: asserts the result without connecting it back to the 180° interior angle sum and the straight-line/supplementary relationship at each vertex, which is what the question asks to prove

questions.topic: algebra with angles at a point
questions.prompt: Four angles meet at a point (forming a full revolution): 4x°, 2x°, 3x° and x°. Write an equation and solve for x.

misconceptions.reason: used the straight-line/triangle total of 180° instead of 360°, forgetting these four angles go all the way around a point (a full revolution)
